critic_raw:
The CGN methodology rests on several interconnected assumptions that deserve scrutiny. I'll organize by root cause.

## 1. The causal independence assumption is unfalsifiable within the evaluation design

**Claim:** Shape, texture, and background are independent causal mechanisms that can be intervened on separately without violating the joint distribution.

**Why it might be wrong:** In natural images, these factors are not independent — bark texture co-occurs with tree-shaped objects; fur texture co-occurs with animal shapes. The CGN's architectural separation imposes statistical independence by construction, but that doesn't mean the factors are causally independent in the data-generating process. When the model 'intervenes' on texture while holding shape fixed, it may produce combinations that are out of support of the natural image distribution (e.g., a dog shape with brick texture). These off-manifold samples could train classifiers to be robust to adversarial combinations rather than genuine distribution shifts.

**Evidence:** Check whether generated counterfactuals (e.g., shape from class A, texture from class B) are rated as semantically plausible by human judges or fall in regions of low density under a pretrained image density model. If FID between counterfactuals and real images is much worse than FID of standard GAN samples on the same classes, the independence assumption is costing fidelity.

## 2. Spurious correlation removal depends on the spurious correlations being exactly those decomposed by the architecture

**Claim:** The CGN decomposition (shape/texture/background) captures the spurious correlations that cause OOD failures in practice.

**Why it might be wrong:** The architecture presupposes which factors are spurious. For ImageNet classifiers, the known texture bias is one failure mode, but real OOD failures are multidimensional (viewpoint, lighting, occlusion, co-occurrence statistics). By augmenting only along the three factored dimensions, the method addresses a specific, hand-selected set of spurious features. Downstream OOD benchmarks that test along those same axes will show improvement almost by construction, while benchmarks testing other axes will show no gain. The evaluation design must be checked: if the OOD test sets used in the paper are ImageNet-C (which tests corruption robustness) or stylized-ImageNet (which tests texture), those are exactly the axes the CGN targets, creating a near-circular evaluation.

**Evidence:** Report performance on OOD benchmarks that are NOT texture/background/shape oriented — e.g., ImageNet-R (rendition shift) or ObjectNet (viewpoint/context shift). If gains disappear on these benchmarks while holding on stylized-ImageNet, the method is fitting the evaluation rather than solving a general robustness problem.

## 3. The mask/shape mechanism conflates shape with segmentation quality, creating signal leakage

**Claim:** The shape mechanism isolates class-relevant shape information.

**Why it might be wrong:** The shape mask in CGN is typically derived from a pretrained segmentation or saliency model. If the segmentation model was trained on the same distribution as the downstream classifier's training set, the 'shape' mechanism encodes not just geometric shape but the segmentation model's class-conditional priors. This means the counterfactual images carry label information through the mask channel, not just through an abstract shape. Classifiers trained on these images may be learning to use the quality or style of the mask itself as a feature, which is a form of signal leakage from a pretrained discriminative model into the generative augmentation pipeline.

**Evidence:** Ablate by training a downstream classifier on the shape masks alone (binary or grayscale). If this classifier achieves substantially above-chance accuracy using only the mask channel, the shape mechanism is leaking discriminative information beyond geometry.

## 4. Evaluating generative quality and downstream robustness with the same generated data conflates generation quality with augmentation utility

**Claim:** Better generative quality (lower FID, higher controllability scores) implies better augmentation for robustness.

**Why it might be wrong:** FID measures distributional proximity to real images. Counterfactual utility for robustness training requires covering combinations that are rare or absent in real data — which by definition will have high FID. There is a fundamental tension: the most useful counterfactuals for breaking spurious correlations are precisely the ones that are least like real images. Optimizing for FID may push the model toward producing realistic but redundant augmentations, while the robustness-relevant counterfactuals are the off-manifold ones that FID penalizes.

**Evidence:** Measure the correlation between FID of the generated distribution and downstream OOD accuracy across CGN variants or training checkpoints. If the best FID checkpoint does not correspond to the best OOD accuracy, the metrics are measuring different things and the generative quality evaluation is not a valid proxy for the augmentation benefit.

## 5. Silent misconfiguration: independent mechanism networks can collapse to copying the input without visible failure

**Claim:** Each mechanism network learns a genuinely distinct factor.

**Why it might be wrong:** If the texture network learns to reproduce the full image (copying background and shape detail) and the shape network learns a trivial mask, the CGN can achieve low reconstruction loss and plausible FID while failing to actually decompose anything. The composed image would still look realistic because the texture network is doing all the work. This is a mode of degenerate training that produces plausible-looking outputs on easy cases (common textures, simple backgrounds) while completely failing the stated goal of disentanglement. Standard GAN training losses don't penalize this collapse.

**Evidence:** Check the mutual information between the texture network output and the shape label, and between the background network output and the foreground object. If the texture network output alone is sufficient to classify the image with near-CGN-training-accuracy, disentanglement has failed. Also inspect whether swapping shape masks between two images of different classes produces a recognizable object of the new class — if not, the shape mechanism isn't carrying class-relevant structure.
all_issues_raised: The independence of shape/texture/background is imposed architecturally rather than reflecting true causal independence, potentially producing off-manifold samples that train classifiers on adversarial rather than realistic variation.
The OOD evaluation benchmarks (e.g., stylized-ImageNet) may be exactly the axes the CGN targets, creating a near-circular evaluation that inflates robustness gains.
The shape mechanism likely leaks discriminative information from a pretrained segmentation model, meaning downstream classifiers may exploit mask style rather than geometry.
FID-based generative quality metrics are in tension with augmentation utility, since the most robustness-relevant counterfactuals are off-manifold and penalized by FID.

The independent mechanism networks can silently collapse to one network doing all the work while still producing plausible-looking images, invalidating the disentanglement claim without visible failure.

The independent mechanism networks can silently collapse to one network doing all the work while still producing plausible-looking images, invalidating the disentanglement claim without visible failure.
